pages.text:
Author Alberto Dian
Title Towards Connected Intelligence: Empowering Robotic Applications with
Agentic AI Frameworks
Degree programme ICT Innovation
Major Autonomous systems and Intelligent Robots
Supervisor Prof. Ville Kyrki
Advisors Dr. Massimiliano Maule, Prof. Davide Brunelli
Collaborative partner Huawei Munich Research Center
Date 21 June 2025 Number of pages 67 Language English
Abstract
This thesis explores the integration of Agentic Artificial Intelligence (Agentic-AI)
frameworks with Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to advance the development
of intelligent, embodied robotic systems. While VLA models offer promising capabil-
ities in enabling robots to understand and execute tasks based on visual and linguistic
inputs, they often lack autonomy and contextual awareness, operating primarily as
reactive systems. To overcome these limitations, this work proposes the AgenticVLA
system, which combines a pretrained and finetuned VLA model with an open-source
agent-based framework. The system is deployed on the Mercury X1 humanoid robot
and is capable of both natural language dialogue and environment-aware task execution.
A custom Virtual Reality (VR) teleoperation platform was developed to collect a
high-quality dual-arm manipulation dataset, which has been used to finetune the VLA
model. The proposed AgenticVLA framework demonstrates improved performance in
task generalization, reasoning under ambiguous prompts, and autonomous decision-
making compared to classical VLA techniques. Experimental evaluations highlight
the benefits of modular agent cooperation, indicating that combining VLA models
with agentic frameworks can improve flexibility and responsiveness in robotic task
execution.
Keywords Embodied AI, Robotics, VLAs, AI Agents, 6G

pages.text:
1 Introduction
In cognitive science and philosophy of mind, embodied cognition highlights the critical
role of the body’s interaction with its environment in shaping thought processes. This
idea extends to Artificial Intelligence (AI) embodiment, which involves giving AI
systems a physical presence in the real world to enhance their perception, sensing, and
interaction with their surroundings. The goal is to improve cognition and understand-
ing, which ultimately leads to better decision making. However, simply being in a
physical form is not enough for true embodiment [1], because embodiment requires
physical grounding: a continuous and bidirectional interaction where the environment
influences the system’s states and vice versa. This highlights the central role of
sensorimotor capabilities, such as sensors for perception and actuators for action,
in achieving meaningful interaction with the environment. The pursuit of Artificial
General Intelligence (AGI), machines with human-like abilities to understand, reason,
and learn, even in unfamiliar situations, remains a central ambition in AI research.
Achieving this vision likely requires embodiment, where AI integrates technologies
such as generative language models, reinforcement learning (RL), edge intelligence,
virtualization, and the internet of senses. Autonomous robots, as embodied AI, could
serve as a platform for this integration, blending these advancements into a cohesive
system capable of sophisticated, context-aware behavior.
Recent advancements in artificial intelligence have spotlighted Vision Language Action
(VLA) models, which are the State-of-the-Art (SoA) in developing generalist robotic
policies for controlling autonomous systems, such as humanoid robots. These models
integrate visual perception, language understanding, and action generation to enable
robots to perform tasks in diverse, real-world environments. Notable contributions
include models like Octo [2], OpenVLA [3], 𝜋0 [4], and Magma [5], which demonstrate
the potential of VLA models in training embodied AI to adapt to unseen scenarios.
Despite their promise, current VLA systems face various limitations. First of all,
only a limited number of open-source datasets are specifically designed for robotics
manipulation purposes (i.e., Open X-Embodiment [6]). In these datasets, most of the
trajectories were performed using single-arm robots, making it almost impossible to
just take this model and deploy them on dual-arms robots, failing on following the
"foundational" paradigm that is fundamental when talking about other models like
Large Language Models (LLMs). When working with new robots, finetuning these
models becomes absolutely necessary, and consequently collecting a custom dataset
with trajectories performed by the studied robot. But even when you have a finetuned
VLA to perform tasks with a specific robot, these models have many limitations. For
example, VLAs are model that wait for the input of the user, those are not able to
choose when to act based on something that is happening in the environment. Most of
the SoA VLAs are not capable of autonomously interact with the environment and
take decisions, but can be a powerful tool if equipped onto entities that have those
capabilities. For this reason, enhancing VLA models with AI agents could support
dynamic interaction with users, environments, and other entities, such as humans or
other AI agents, could lead to more robust embodied AI systems. Agentic AI [7]
7

pages.text:
represent a significant leap forward, distinguished by their ability to autonomously
set and pursue complex goals in unpredictable settings through independent resource
management. Driven by the need for systems that can navigate complex real-world
challenges with flexibility, Agentic AIs shift AI’s role from passive, reactive functions
to proactive strategic planning, information processing, and problem-solving, unlock-
ing transformative potential across applications [8].
For these reasons, this work focuses on combining two already existing and promising
field of AI research, VLAs and AI Agents, to create a single physical entity [9], a
humanoid robot, capable of interacting with the environment in multiple ways, such as
speaking with a human or picking up an object. This could lead to better autonomous
systems, a step towards achieving AGI.
1.1 Research Questions
RQ1: Can an Agentic AI Framework be combined with Visual-Language-Action-
Model towards achieving AGI?
This question examines whether mixing an Agentic AI Framework with VLA models
can improve reasoning capabilities, multimodal perception, and adaptive action,
thereby achieving a higher level of intelligence.
RQ2: Can a network of AI Agents improve the perception and decision-making
capabilities of autonomous robots in a collaborative task setting? If so, how?
This question investigates whether a network of AI agents can enhance the perceptual
accuracy and decision-making efficiency of autonomous robots. It studies the benefits
that come from a collaborative environment composed of AI Agents equipped with
different scopes and tools, also focusing on current network infrastructures limitations.
1.2 Thesis Objectives
This thesis explores how the integration of Agentic AI frameworks with VLA models
can enhance the overall intelligence and adaptability of embodied AI systems. While
VLA models enable robots to interpret and act on visual, linguistic, and motor inputs,
they often lack autonomy and contextual awareness, relying on external prompts and
specific datasets, as discussed previously in the Introduction section. This research in-
vestigates whether embedding these models within a broader Agentic AI framework can
address such limitations, supporting more flexible, proactive, and goal-driven behavior.
In addition, the thesis examines how networks of specialized AI agents, each focused
on tasks such as perception, reasoning, or planning, can collaborate to improve a
robot’s ability to operate effectively in complex, real-world environments. By studying
multi-agent cooperation and the system-level coordination it enables, this work aims
to understand how distributed intelligence can lead to more accurate perception, better
decision-making, and more robust task execution.

pages.text:
Through this dual focus, the study seeks to provide practical insights into the design
of intelligent and embodied systems that are capable of both individual autonomy
and collaborative behavior, with potential applications in robotics, automation, and
human-AI interaction.
1.3 Thesis Structure
This thesis is organized to follow the chronological development of the project over
a six-month period. After the background chapter, which provides the theoretical
foundation necessary to understand the approach and results, the methodology chapter
outlines the steps taken to set up the experimental environment. The project is
structured into four main phases:
1. Teleoperation: A custom teleoperation system was developed to control the
Mercury X1 [10] humanoid robot using the Meta Quest 3 headset [11]. This
setup enabled immersive, real-time control of the robot for data generation
purposes.
2. Data collection: In this phase, a balanced dataset of robotic trajectories was
collected using the teleoperation system. The goal was to create a diverse
and representative dataset tailored to the capabilities and configuration of the
Mercury X1 robot.
3. Training the VLA: The collected dataset was used to finetune the OpenVLA-
OFT [12] model, adapting it to the specific embodiment and task set of the
robot. This phase also included preliminary testing in real-world scenarios to
assess performance.
4. Agentic AI integration: Finally, the VLA model was integrated into an
Agentic AI framework, combining it with the AutoGen [13] architecture. This
phase focused on studying the behavior of the resulting system, referred to as
the AgenticVLA, by analyzing its ability to reason, adapt, and interact more
autonomously and operate under complex tasks, beyond the baseline VLA
knowledges.
Following the methodology, the thesis presents the experimental evaluation. To address
both research questions, a comparative analysis is conducted between the OpenVLA
model, the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT model and the proposed AgenticVLA system.
These experiments examine how each system responds to variations in task phrasing
and context, using metrics such as Success Rate (SR) to quantify improvements in
reasoning, adaptability, and overall task performance.

pages.text:
2 State of The Art and Background Overview
This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of the current SoA and foundational
background relevant to the integration of Agentic AI frameworks with VLA models for
robotic applications. It explores key advancements in VLA models, such as OpenVLA
and OpenVLA-OFT, which enable robots to process multimodal inputs for task
execution. Additionally, it goes deeper into the principles of Agentic AI, highlighting
frameworks like AutoGen that facilitate autonomous, goal-driven behavior through
multi-agent collaboration. The chapter also introduces the Mercury X1 humanoid
robot, the experimental platform used in this study, and discusses its design and
technical capabilities. Furthermore, it examines the role of robotic teleoperation,
including virtual reality interfaces and software tools like ROS# [14] and Unity [15], in
data collection and system control. Finally, the potential of 6G networking to support
distributed AI agent coordination is addressed, setting the stage for understanding the
technological landscape and challenges tackled in this thesis.
2.1 Visual-Language-Action Models
VLAs represent a class of multimodal systems within embodied AI, designed to
process and integrate information from vision and language inputs, and generate
corresponding actions. In language-conditioned robotic tasks, these models enable
robots to interpret language instructions, visually perceive their environment, and
take appropriate actions, combining the strengths of these different modalities. VLAs
offer promising capabilities compared to traditional deep learning approaches for
policy learning, such as deep reinforcement learning and imitation learning. They
are particularly appealing for their potential to improve generalization across tasks
and adaptability to varied environments. While VLAs show promise in improving
versatility and generalization, their capabilities, particularly in terms of dexterity in
the robotic sense, such as dexterous manipulation, are still under active investigation.
As VLAs are a relatively new approach, further validation in real-world applications
is needed to fully assess their effectiveness in these areas. A notable challenge in
this field is the development of more versatile multitask policies, similar to recent
advancements in large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs).
Creating such policies requires training on a diverse set of tasks to ensure adaptability
across environments. Another challenge lies in task specification: enabling users
to express desired robot behavior through natural language remains a key objective.
Addressing this requires effective language-conditioned policies that can interpret and
act upon linguistic instructions. Building upon the successes of large VLMs, VLAs
are well-suited to tackle these challenges. They typically use vision foundation models
as encoders to derive latent visual representations, which are expected to capture
factors such as object class, pose, and geometry, from the current environment. The
models then encode instructions using token embeddings from LLMs and employ
strategies to align both vision and language representations. Finetuning on robot-
specific data allows the LLM to function as a control policy, making decisions based
on the multimodal inputs. This fusion of language, vision, and action defines VLAs

pages.text:
within embodied AI and represents a step toward more capable and general-purpose
robotic agents. In the realm of control policies, VLAs excel at generating low-level
actions, such as translation and rotation offsets, by processing language instructions
and visual input. In contrast, higher-level task planners decompose long-horizon
tasks into subtasks, which guide the robot toward achieving an overall goal. Many
current robotic systems adopt such hierarchical models, where high-level planners
leverage powerful models, and low-level control policies focus on precision and speed,
mirroring the approach in hierarchical reinforcement learning.
Building on the VLAs paradigm, several works have proposed robotic Foundation
Models (FM) that convert robot observations, such as images and joint states, into
actions and provide zero or few-shot generalization to new environments or robot.
These Generalist Robot Policies (GRPs) are usually trained or finetuned on the Open
X-Embodiment dataset, the largest robot manipulation dataset up to date. This dataset
containes almost 1M+ real robot trajectories spanning 22 robot embodiments, from
single robot arms to bi-manual robots and quadrupeds. The dataset was constructed by
pooling 60 existing robot datasets from 34 robotic research labs around the world and
converting them into a consistent data format for easy download and usage. To evaluate
how much OpenX-Embodiment training can improve the performance of learned
policies on individual robots, they introduces two transformer-based robotic policies:
RT-1 [16] and RT-2 [17]. RT-1 is a 35M parameter network based on a transformer
architecture, designed for robotic control. It processes a history of 15 images alongside
natural language input. Each image is passed through an ImageNet-pretrained Efficient-
Net [18] model, while the natural language instruction is converted into an embedding
using the Universal Sentence Encoder. The visual and language representations are
then combined using Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) [19] layers, resulting in
81 vision-language tokens. These tokens are input into a decoder-only transformer,
which generates the tokenized actions. RT-2 is a family of large vision-language-action
models trained on extensive datasets spanning vision, language, and robotic control.
This approach allows pretrained vision-language models to be adapted for robotic
control, leveraging their foundational capabilities and transferring some of their gen-
eralization strengths. Although these models mark significant progress, they remain
limited in several key areas. They often constrain users to a predefined and narrow set
of input data, such as a single camera stream, and they lack effective mechanisms for
finetuning to new domains. Perhaps most importantly, the largest versions of these
models are not publicly accessible.
An important contribution to this field is Octo [2], a transformer-based policy pretrained
on the Open X-Embodiment dataset, fully open-source. Octo combines components
that have already been discussed in other works, like a transformer backbone, support
for both language and goal image specification, and a diffusion head to model expres-
sive action distributions, creating a powerful generalist robot policy. The developers
demonstrated that Octo serves as a versatile policy initialization that can be effectively
finetuned to new observation and action spaces.

In the context of my thesis, I chose to focus specifically on OpenVLA [3] and its
improved variant OpenVLA-OFT [12] for several compelling reasons. First, during
the development of this work, OpenVLA was a relatively new contribution, yet it
had already gained significant attention and validation through citations in multiple
peer-reviewed papers, lending credibility to its design and performance claims. Second,
unlike many large-scale vision-language-action models that remain closed-source,
OpenVLA is fully open-source and comes with detailed documentation, making it
accessible and practical for academic research and experimentation. Lastly, the release
of OpenVLA-OFT introduced several architectural and performance improvements
that directly addressed common limitations in VLA models, aligning well with the
objectives of this thesis in studying scalable, efficient, and generalizable robot control
policies.
2.1.1 OpenVLA
OpenVLA [3] is a 7B-parameter open-source VLA that pushes the boundaries of
generalist robot manipulation. It is built on a pretrained, visually-conditioned language
model backbone that captures visual features at various granularities, and it has been
finetuned on a large and diverse dataset of 970k robot manipulation trajectories from
the Open X-Embodiment dataset. One of the key contributions of OpenVLA is its
finetuning strategy, which has not been extensively explored in previous work. The
model outperforms even larger models, such as the 55B-parameter RT-2-X, and shows
significant improvement over other finetuned policies like Octo, as well as from-scratch
imitation learning using diffusion policies. In particular, OpenVLA excels at tasks that
involve grounding language to behavior in multi-task settings with multiple objects.

standard design of recent VLMs, which generally consist of three parts: (1) a visual
encoder that processes image inputs into image patch embeddings, (2) a projector
that maps these visual embeddings into the input space of a language model, and
(3) a LLM backbone. During training, these models learn end-to-end using a text
token prediction objective based on paired or interleaved vision-language data curated

pages.text:
from various online sources. OpenVLA specifically extends the Prismatic-7B VLM,
which uses a 600M-parameter visual encoder, a two-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron
(MLP) projector, and a 7B-parameter Llama 2 language model backbone. The visual
encoder is a two-part system, combining pretrained SigLIP and DinoV2 models. This
combination of visual features helps improve spatial reasoning, which is critical for
tasks like robot control.
For training OpenVLA, the action prediction problem is framed as a vision-language
task where an image and a natural language instruction are mapped to a sequence
of robot actions. The continuous robot actions are discretized into discrete tokens,
which the language model can predict. Each action dimension is quantized into one
of 256 bins, with bin widths based on the quantiles of the training data rather than
the min-max range, which helps avoid issues with outlier actions that could affect
discretization quality. Finally, the resolution of input images plays a significant role in
training efficiency. Higher-resolution images, while providing more tokens and longer
context lengths, also demand more computational power, with little performance gain
when compared to using 224x224px images, even though the latter is more efficient
during training.
2.1.2 OpenVLA-OFT
OpenVLA-OFT [12] is an improved version of OpenVLA, developed by the same
research team, that focuses on improving the finetuning and inference performaces
of the VLA (see Figure 2). They replaced the autoregressive decoding technique
from the base model to a parallel decoding approach, which leverages bidirectional
attention and generates all actions in a single forward pass. This approach naturally
extends to action chunking, predicting the next K actions at each timestep, achieving
43x faster throughput than base OpenVLA. Additionally, OpenVLA-OFT is capable
of processing multiple camera views and low-dimensional robot state inputs, such as
joint angles and gripper state. All input embeddings are concatenated before being
passed to the decoder, creating a unified latent representation, to enable the model
to generate actions considering all the available information. This architecture can
efficiently digest multimodal inputs and work with dual-arms robots, as the researchers
show successful experiments conducted with the ALOHA robot [20].

decisions and execute actions to achieve a specific goal [7]. Compared to traditional
AI models, AI agents have the following unique features [21]:
1. Proactive interaction with environments: AI agents can actively interact with
the environment, seeking for information, initiating actions to achieve a desired
goal.
2. Goal-oriented self-learning and self-correction: AI Agents can leverage

The development of increasingly powerful LLMs led to a surge of LLM-based au-

making capabilities. These AI Agents can also offer natural language interfaces for
human interaction, providing greater flexibility and enhanced explainability.
Selecting the appropriate framework for developing agentic AI is critical to ensure
scalability, completeness and alignment with intended objectives. Currently, the most
popular frameworks are AutoGen, LangChain [22], CrewAI [23] or SmolAgents [24],
each one with different characteristics and specifics.
Agent design foundation
In its most fundamental form, an agent consists of three core components (see Figure
3) that work together to enable intelligent behavior and task execution:
1. Model: The model is the foundational brain of the agent, typically a large lan-
guage model (LLM), responsible for reasoning, understanding, and generating
responses. It interprets user inputs, maintains context, and makes informed
decisions based on learned patterns and knowledge. The quality and capabili-
ties of the model directly influence the agent’s intelligence, adaptability, and
performance across a wide range of tasks.
2. Tools: Tools are external functions, Application Programming Interfaces
(APIs), or plugins that the agent can invoke to perform actions beyond its
internal capabilities. These may include web search, database queries, image
generation, or executing code. Tools expand the agent’s functionality, enabling

2.3.1 Design and Structure
The Mercury X1 is composed of two primary components, shown in Figure 5:
the Mercury B1 dual-arm semi-humanoid robot and a robust wheeled mobile base.
This hybrid design enables the robot to perform tasks requiring both mobility and
manipulation, distinguishing it from traditional stationary robotic arms or purely
mobile platforms. The robot features a total of 19 degrees of freedom (DOF), with
each of its two seven-axis arms contributing significantly to its dexterity. The arms are
constructed with a lightweight carbon fiber shell, which reduces the overall weight
to 55 kg while maintaining structural rigidity and durability. This design choice
enhances the robot’s power efficiency and payload capacity, rated at a maximum of 1
kg. The mobile base is equipped with high-performance direct-drive motors, allowing
the Mercury X1 to achieve a maximum operating speed of 1.2 m/s and navigate
inclines up to 15 degrees or obstacles up to 2 cm in height. The base integrates
advanced perception systems, including high-performance LiDAR, ultrasonic sensors,
and 2D vision capabilities, enabling robust environmental awareness and autonomous
navigation. These features make the Mercury X1 particularly suited for dynamic
environments where adaptability and real-time decision-making are critical.

2.4 Robotic Teleoperation Design
Robotic teleoperation refers to the remote control of a robot by a human operator,
where commands are transmitted to the robot and sensor data, such as video, posi-
tional feedback, or environmental readings, is relayed back to the operator through
a communication channel [28]. Unlike fully autonomous systems, which aim to
operate independently, teleoperation leverages human intelligence to guide robots in
tasks where automation alone is insufficient. This approach emerged in the mid-20th
century, with early examples like the teleoperated robotic arms used for handling
radioactive materials. Since then, teleoperation has evolved into a vital technology
for extending human capabilities into environments that are distant, hazardous, or
otherwise inaccessible. Teleoperation is essential not only for real-time control but
also for collecting the demonstration data required to train robust autonomous poli-
cies, addressing the gap where full autonomy remains elusive due to environmental
complexity or technological limitations.
Teleoperation’s evolution has been shaped by the interfaces used to bridge human
operators and robotic systems. Early systems relied on rudimentary interfaces, such as
joysticks and control panels, which offered limited situational awareness and required
significant operator training. As communication technologies advanced, teleoperation
over packet-switched networks, notably the Internet, became feasible by the mid-1990s.
A pioneering example is [29], which demonstrated remote control of a robot arm
through a web browser, highlighting the potential and challenges, like latency and
packet loss, of Internet-based teleoperation. These challenges persist, driving research
into more robust communication protocols and interfaces. The choice of interface
is critical to teleoperation’s effectiveness, influencing operator performance, task
efficiency, and system reliability. Traditional interfaces have gradually given way
to more intuitive options, such as Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) with real-time
video feeds, haptic devices providing force feedback, and, more recently, immersive
technologies. Each advancement aims to enhance situational awareness and reduce
cognitive load, enabling operators to manage complex tasks seamlessly. For example,
author in [30] highlights the importance of interface design in teleoperation systems,
emphasizing that the human-machine interface must be designed to support perceptual
and cognitive needs. He discusses how interface elements, such as visual feedback,
control latency, and haptic cues, affect human supervisory control, and argues that
well-designed interfaces are essential for maintaining operator engagement, especially
in scenarios where partial autonomy is combined with manual override.
Among these developments, Virtual Reality (VR) has emerged as a transformative
interface for teleoperation, offering an immersive environment that replicates the
robot’s perspective and surroundings. By integrating VR, operators can interact with
mobile robots more naturally [31], overcoming the limitations of earlier interfaces and
addressing the growing demands of applications like exploration, surveillance, and
environmental monitoring.
18

2.4.1 Virtual Reality Frameworks
In VR environments, operators can remotely guide and monitor robots, overcoming
the limitations of traditional human-machine interfaces [32]. This not only reduces
the need for physical presence on-site but also enhances safety, especially in haz-
ardous industrial environments. The combination of VR and robotics allows for more
complex tasks, such as precise manipulation of objects in challenging settings, to
be performed with exceptional dexterity and flexibility. This technological synergy
has wide-reaching benefits across various industries, including manufacturing and
logistics, where seamless collaboration between human operators and robotic systems
boosts both productivity and operational performance.
Many examples prove that this technology extends robotic controls, such as the
use of the HTC Vive Pro 2 [33] headset to control the KUKA youBot robot [34].
This system leverages VR to enable intuitive teleoperation of a mobile manipulator,
demonstrating how immersive interfaces can improve operator awareness and con-
trol responsiveness in dynamic environments. Furthermore, teleoperation’s role in
data collection is exemplified by systems like in [35]. This framework utilizes VR
controllers, RGB-D cameras, and other interfaces to teleoperate mobile manipulators
like PAL Tiago++ [36], Toyota HSR [37], and Fetch [38], both in simulation and the
real world. TeleMoMa’s modular design lowers the barrier for collecting high-quality
demonstrations, which are then used to train imitation learning policies for tasks
requiring synchronized whole-body motion. This highlights teleoperation’s dual
purpose: enabling immediate control and generating datasets critical for advancing
autonomy in mobile robotics.
Similarly, recent advancements have pushed the boundaries of VR-based teleoperation
further, like OPEN TEACH [39], an open-source framework that utilizes the Meta
Quest 3 VR headset to control various robots, including mobile manipulators, multi-
fingered hands, and bimanual arms. This system operates at up to 90Hz with
smooth visual feedback, allowing operators to perform tasks ranging from tabletop
manipulation to contact-rich dexterous operations across both simulated and real-world
settings. Its plug-and-play design and support for diverse robot morphologies make it
a significant step toward accessible and flexible teleoperation, particularly for mobile
robot applications in remote and dynamic contexts. Another notable contribution is
AnyTeleop [40], which offers a unified VR-based teleoperation framework adaptable to
multiple robot arms, hands, and environments. By employing vision-based control and
flexible camera configurations, AnyTeleop supports dexterous manipulation tasks in
both real and simulated realities, outperforming systems tailored to specific hardware.
Its applicability to mobile manipulators and its emphasis on scalability address
some of the persistent challenges in teleoperation over packet-switched networks,
such as adaptability to varying conditions and hardware. Together, these systems
exemplify how VR enhances teleoperation by providing intuitive, adaptable, and
high-performance interfaces, aligning with the growing need for robust teleoperation
technologies in fields like exploration, mining, and surveillance, while also serving as

2.4.2 ROS#
Figure 6: ROS# Pipeline. Source: [14]
ROS# is a set of open-source software libraries and tools in C# for communicating
with ROS from .NET applications, in particular Unity (see Figure 6). With ROS#,
developers can effortlessly create .NET applications that communicate with ROS
nodes, subscribe to and publish topics, handle actions and services, and interact with
ROS messages. This enables the development of robotics applications, simulations,
and automation systems within the .NET ecosystem.
2.4.3 Unity
Unity is a powerful cross-platform game engine widely adopted not only for video
game development but also for applications in simulation, robotics, and virtual reality
(VR). With a flexible architecture and extensive toolsets for 3D modeling, physics sim-
ulation, and user interface design, Unity has become an increasingly popular choice for
researchers and developers working on embodied AI and robotic teleoperation systems.
In the context of this thesis, Unity plays a central role in the teleoperation interface
used to control the Mercury X1 robot. Its support for C# scripting and integration
capabilities with external libraries, particularly ROS#, make it a highly effective
platform for interfacing with real-world robots in a virtual environment. This enables
the visualization of real-time sensor data and camera streams, immersive human-in-
the-loop control, and collection of demonstration data for training AI models.
Unity was selected for this project due to the following key capabilities:
• High-quality rendering: Unity’s rendering engine allows for photorealistic
visualization of the robot and its environment, improving operator immersion
and environmental awareness.
• Native VR support: Unity provides built-in support for VR headsets such as
Meta Quest 3, enabling intuitive teleoperation via motion-tracked controllers.

• Extensibility: The ability to integrate ROS topics and services through ROS#
allows Unity to act as a middleware bridge between the operator and the robotic
system.
• Real-time interaction: Unity’s game loop architecture ensures low-latency,
event-driven control, which is crucial in robotic applications requiring precision
and responsiveness.
Through Unity, operators can not only observe but also interact with the robot in an
intuitive and user-friendly interface, using hand gestures and controller inputs. The
interface is also designed to display telemetry data and trigger the data recording system,
making it a central hub for both robot control and dataset collection. By combining
Unity’s immersive visualization and interactivity with the hardware capabilities of the
Mercury X1 robot, the resulting teleoperation and simulation platform creates a hybrid
reality interface where AI models can be trained and evaluated in a human-centric
manner.
2.5 The role of 6G powered systems
In June 2023, the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) [41] finalized the
framework for the 6G Vision, addressing the fundamental question, "What is 6G?" from
two key perspectives [42]. The first focuses on the evolution of mobile communications,
emphasizing Ultra-Reliable and Low-Latency Communications (URLLC) to enable
and support large-scale connectivity. The second perspective extends beyond traditional
mobile communications, aiming to achieve Integrated Sensing and Communication
(ISAC) while leveraging AI and networking.
Figure 7: Connected Intelligence = AGI for 6G + 6G for AGI. Source: [42]
A central aspect of 6G is its ability to support large-scale AI-driven services and
applications. In the era of AI, the core components of applications and services
will increasingly rely on AI agents, intelligent entities capable of perceiving their
environment and autonomously making decisions (see Figure 7). These agents can
acquire knowledge through continuous sensing and interaction with other agents,
facilitating the exchange of valuable information to accomplish assigned tasks. The
ultimate vision is to establish Connected Intelligence, where diverse AI agents, such
as autonomous robots and sensing entities, collaborate within an intelligent network

to achieve collective objectives. Embodied AI, particularly autonomous robots, plays
a crucial role in realizing this vision. Their integration within intelligent networks
enables seamless coordination with other agents for task planning and execution across
various environments, including domestic spaces or road systems where autonomous
vehicles operate [43].
2.6 6G for Robotics
The integration of 6G communication technologies with robotics is expected to
reshape the design and operation of autonomous systems across a variety of domains.
Unlike previous generations of mobile networks, 6G is envisioned not only as a
communication platform but also as an enabler of real-time, distributed intelligence
for AI-powered agents [44, 45]. Major standardization bodies such as the Interna-
tional Telecommunication Union (ITU) and 3GPP have identified robotics as a key
vertical for 6G, emphasizing URLLC, ISAC, and semantic-aware data exchange as
foundational enablers for collaborative robotic systems [45, 46]. These capabilities
are critical for applications such as cooperative manipulation, swarm robotics, and
human-robot teaming in dynamic or unstructured environments. According to [43],
6G will support multi-modal and context-aware robotic coordination by combining
high-bandwidth sensing with AI-based planning, thereby improving both precision and
adaptability. Their work emphasizes that robotic networks will increasingly benefit
from “network-native intelligence,” where communication, computation, and sensing
are co-designed. Emerging technologies such as the tactile internet and Extended
Reality (XR), when powered by 6G, are expected to enable immersive and high-fidelity
remote interaction with robotic systems [47]. This includes real-time 3D hologra-
phy, haptics, and spatial audio, which are relevant for domains like remote surgery,
disaster response, and industrial inspection. Such capabilities demand low-latency,
high-throughput networks, which exceed the capacity of current 5G systems in many
practical settings.
A vision proposed by Huawei [48] introduces the concept of meta-level autonomy
in robotics, in which robots can autonomously define tasks, detect environmental
changes, and adapt behavior using foundational AI models and shared knowledge.
Achieving this level of autonomy will require persistent connectivity, decentralized
learning, and integration with other AI agents via 6G infrastructure. Finally, the
modular agent-based structure explored in this thesis aligns with recent proposals
for deploying lightweight, specialized AI agents at the network edge. These agents
can collaborate dynamically across heterogeneous hardware and environments, an
approach compatible with 6G’s goal of enabling distributed AI-as-a-Service and
intent-based orchestration of cyber-physical systems [45, 46].
Taken together, the convergence of robotics and 6G is not merely about enhanced
connectivity, but about enabling a new class of intelligent, reactive, and collaborative
robotic systems. Nevertheless, open challenges remain around energy efficiency,
secure agent coordination, and reliability in distributed deployments, all of which are

2.6.1 Agentic AI for 6G Networking
Major Standardization Development Organizations (SDOs), including 3GPP [49] and
ITU-T , have widely recognized the role of Agentic AI in communication networks
as a pivotal trend shaping the future 6G networking systems. A proof of this trend
is illustrated in ETSI GR ENI 051 [50], where a novel architecture and functionality
of a multi agent AI Core for End-to-End (E2E) network slicing is presented. In this
view, multiple specialized AI agents within the core can autonomously assemble
network/app functions and dynamically manage slice lifecycle, using high level
intents (even in natural language) communication mechanisms without tenant-specific
technical configurations. Unlike classical AI approaches, Agentic AI systems for
networking are envisioned to be capable to take autonomous and independent actions
in close loop with the environment, bypassing the current open challenges in terms
of [51]:
1. Network capacity: Traditional networks are ill-equipped to handle the surging,
heterogeneous data demands of AI-driven services. 6G networks, with integrated
support for semantic communication, network slicing, and AI-native control
planes, enable fine-grained, task-oriented data transmission. This technology
empowers agentic AI systems to operate as intelligent service orchestrators,
capable of filtering, abstracting, and prioritizing information flows.
2. AI model flexibility and dynamic contextualization: Legacy AI models are
centralized and static, struggling to adapt to user mobility, device diversity,
or dynamic context changes. 6G introduces intent-based networking (IBN),
enabling Agentic AI entities to engage in continual learning, infer real-time user
intent, and autonomously coordinate services across devices and domains.
3. Rapid deployment and AI scalability: Foundational AI models (e.g., LLMs,
vision transformers) are resource-intensive and ill-suited for deployment at
the edge or in mission-critical scenarios. 6G’s compute-aware architecture,
including AI-as-a-service (AIaaS) at the edge and in-network computing, allows
deployment of small-scale, specialized agentic models. These agents collaborate
asynchronously to achieve distributed intelligence, adapt incrementally, and
scale elastically with task complexity.
The evolution of mobile communication systems from International Mobile Telecom-
munications 2020 (IMT-2020) to Agentic-based 6G networking [52] [53] is envisioned
to be transformative, particularly for future applications (i.e., Robotics). While 5G
introduced significant improvements in terms of throughput, latency, and reliability,
early market deployments and research findings have highlighted specific challenges
that need to be addressed to fully realize next-generation services. These include
supporting extremely high device density, ensuring consistent quality of service across
diverse environments, and seamlessly integrating cutting-edge technologies such as

3 Research Methodology
This chapter outlines the methodological framework adopted to achieve the research
objectives and address the stated research questions. It is structured into four key
phases, each representing a critical step in the design, development, and evaluation
of an intelligent embodied system based on the Mercury X1 humanoid robot. The
overall approach follows a bottom-up strategy, beginning with low-level control and
data acquisition, moving through model training, and culminating in high-level system
integration within an agentic architecture.
The first section details the development of a custom teleoperation framework, designed
to provide intuitive and immersive control of the Mercury X1 robot using a virtual
reality interface. This system served not only as a control interface but also as a data
collection tool capable of capturing high-quality, human-like robot trajectories. These
demonstrations formed the foundation for the subsequent phase.
Figure 8: Methodology: an overview of each step of the process.
Following the teleoperation setup, the next phase involved collecting a dataset of
task demonstrations tailored to the robot’s dual-arm configuration and sensor setup.
Particular care was taken to ensure that the data was both representative and diverse,
while maintaining task specificity to support effective model learning. The collected
dataset was formatted according to standard reinforcement learning conventions,
allowing it to be used directly with modern VLA models.
With the dataset in place, the third phase focused on finetuning an open-source VLA
model, OpenVLA-OFT, on the collected trajectories. This step aimed to train a
generalist robotic policy capable of interpreting language instructions and executing
corresponding actions with the Mercury X1. The training process required specific
adjustments to input formats, hyperparameters, and architecture components to ac-
commodate the dual-arm control and multi-view inputs.
The final section of the methodology presents the integration of the finetuned VLA

model into a broader Agentic AI framework. By combining reactive control via
OpenVLA-OFT with goal-driven planning and reasoning via the AutoGen frame-
work, the resulting system, termed the AgenticVLA, was capable of autonomous
decision-making, multi-agent coordination, and contextual interaction. This full-stack
integration was designed to evaluate the feasibility of combining VLAs with agentic
architectures in pursuit of more intelligent, embodied AI systems.
Together, these four phases build a coherent pipeline from data collection to embodied
autonomy, forming the experimental foundation for the results and analysis that follow.

pages.text:
3.1 Robotic Raw Data Acquisition Procedure
To collect high-quality robotic trajectories, a custom teleoperation system was devel-
oped. The system allows a human operator to control the Mercury X1 humanoid robot
in real-time using immersive VR hardware. This setup not only enabled intuitive robot
manipulation but also served as a foundation for collecting training data essential for
finetuning the VLA model. The main design goals were real-time responsiveness,
intuitive controls, and seamless data logging for subsequent training.
3.1.1 Robotic Hardware and Software configuration
The teleoperation system is based on the Mercury X1 humanoid robot, equipped with
dual seven-axis arms and multiple onboard cameras. The Meta Quest 3 headset and
its hand controllers were used to enable immersive control. Three video feeds were
captured: one from the main head-mounted camera, and two extra USB cameras, one
capturing the scene from a top-down prespective and the second one from a third-view
point of view. The head-mounted camera is an Orbbec Deeyea 3D camera, while the
two USB cameras are both Logitech C920 HD Webcams. The system operated over a
wireless local network, ensuring low-latency data transmission between the control
interface and the robot.
The control interface was built in Unity using C#. To bridge Unity with the robot’s
Robot Operating System (ROS), the ROS# library was used. This allowed real-time
streaming of sensor data and commands. The Meta Quest 3 controllers were interfaced
via Meta’s OVRInput library, enabling tracking of 6-DOF poses and button states. On
the robot side, custom Python scripts consumed these messages and translated them
into control signals using the pymycobot library.

pages.text:
3.2 Dataset structure Design
In this section, the data collection phase is introduced. The creation of a custom dataset
was a fundamental step for multiple reasons. First of all, as said in the introduction
chapter, most of the open-source VLA models are trained on the same dataset, which is
the Open X-Embodiment dataset. This set contains a large number of robot trajectories
performed using different robots with specific setups, meaning that cameras, lighting
conditions and tasks performed can vary significantly from one sub-set to another.
In addition, this dataset lacks the dual-arm interaction capabilities required by the
Mercury X1 humanoid robot. For these reasons, when working with models such as
VLAs applied for robotic applications, it is crucial to collect a custom dataset with your
own setup and specifics in order to train or finetune these models correctly. Following
what researchers consider good practice, the data collection phase prioritized the
quality and variability of task execution, without the need to collect large amount
of data. This strategy was aimed at maximizing generalization while minimizing
overfitting, ensuring that the trained model could adapt to unseen scenarios involving
similar objects and goals.
3.2.1 Robotic Task Structure
The Mercury X1 dataset was designed to be simple yet comprehensive, introducing
small variations for each recorded task to ensure diversity and sparsity within the data.
This approach was informed by numerous attempts to create different versions of the
dataset, varying camera positions and task designs. The main action performed by the
robot in each episode is the pick and place task, where the robot interacts with objects
visible in front of it, using one of its two arms to pick up an object and place it on a
designated area marked by a plate. Throughout all recorded tasks, only one arm, the
one closest to the object, performs the movement, while the other arm remains in its
original position, moving only slightly to avoid collisions (see Figure 10). The robot
remains stationary for the duration of each episode.
Each task is described with a specific text instruction that is then fed to the system to
learn correlations between spatial and language features. This dataset is composed of
one main text instruction:
• Put the [color] [object] on the [color] plate : Ex. "put the yellow banana on
the red plate"
This choice follows guidelines from the developers of OpenVLA-OFT and draws
inspiration from the Open X-Embodiment dataset, where tasks are simple and concise,
and small variations are introduced by interacting with different objects to perform the
same task. In the Mercury X1 dataset, two objects are considered: a yellow banana
and purple grapes. Additionally, other objects such as an apple and an orange are
visible in the scene during each episode, but no interaction is performed with them.
The entire dataset consists of 480 episodes, a size that aligns with the experiments
proposed on the ALOHA dataset by the OpenVLA-OFT researchers. Another layer of
sparsity was introduced by varying the position from which the object is picked up. As

3.2.2 Data acquisition process
Data collection was conducted in real-time using a VR-based teleoperation system.
The Meta Quest 3 controllers captured the operator’s movements, which were mirrored
by the robot in a closed-loop system running at 50 Hz. To record the demonstrations
for training, each trajectory was sampled at a consistent frame rate of 25 Hz, following
the standard set by the ALOHA dataset, which features a similar bi-manual robot
architecture. Each episode consists of a series of steps, representing the smallest units
of action.
Each step contains:
1. RGB images from three synchronized camera views (main head camera, top-
down camera, third-view camera).
2. Joint angles for both arms (14-dimensional vector: 7 per arm)
3. Gripper actuation values.
4. End-effector position (X-Y-Z coordinates with respect to the robot reference
system), 3 per arm, 6 in total.
5. Timestamps and status flags.
The USB cameras were positioned strategically to capture information from different
perspectives. Specifically, the top-down camera was mounted above the robot’s head
using a small tripod to capture both arms from above the scene. The third-view camera,
on the other hand, captures the entire table with all the objects as well as the robot.
32

A fundamental consideration when deciding the cameras’ points of view is ensuring
that the robot’s end effectors are always visible in the captured images to maintain
consistency in the collected data. This setup choice was guided by studying and
observing other datasets within the Open X-Embodiment collection. It became clear
that capturing the entire scene with a wide shot, represented here by the previously
mentioned third-view camera, is necessary, while a second view typically focuses on
the end-effector’s wrist position and orientation (see Figure 12).
Figure 12: Cameras setup used to record robotic trajectories.
Another common setup, as seen in the ALOHA dataset, uses one wide camera and two
wrist cameras. During the development of this work, that setup was considered and
even implemented (see Figure 9), but the increased complexity of the dataset did not
yield the desired results. After careful evaluation, data was collected using a top-down
camera, a third-view camera, and the main camera mounted directly on the robot.

pages.text:
3.2.3 Dataset Structure and Format
During data collection, each episode, which corresponds to one completed task, was
stored in a folder directly on the Meta Quest 3 memory system. The data follows the
Reinforcement Learning Dataset (RLDS) [55] format, where every episode consists of
a series of steps sampled at a precise frequency during recording. This format is widely
used to represent robotic manipulation trajectories and is the same one employed
in the Open X-Embodiment dataset. Due to its popularity, RLDS is supported by
the community with custom scripts to convert data into other formats, such as the
Lerobot dataset, a flexible and interesting framework developed by HuggingFace [56].
Each episode’s folder contains three subfolders with frames recorded from the three
cameras involved, along with a JSON file containing metadata and information about
end-effector positions, states, and boolean flags indicating the start and end of the
episode.
The JSON file follows the structure shown in Table 6. Each image is encoded in
PNG format using the RGB color scheme and has a resolution of 640x480 pixels. In
the metadata of each episode, the high level task field contains the text instruction
related to the task, while the success boolean indicates whether the task was completed
successfully. Each step is stored within the steps field and includes: observation
data with paths to images and a 14-dimensional joint states array, a 10-dimensional
action array, and boolean values marking the start and end of the episode. The action
vector is 10-dimensional because rotation information for each end effector was not
recorded, as it was fixed to zero. This decision was made to ensure smoother robot
control and reduce data complexity. Instead, what is stored are the end-effector
positions and gripper values for each arm (4 values per arm), along with the linear and
angular velocity of the robot’s wheeled base. Importantly, the end-effector positions
are expressed in the robot base reference frame, meaning that the origin (0,0,0)
corresponds to the position of the robot’s wheels.
34

pages.text:
3.3 Baseline VLA model finetuning
OpenVLA-OFT has been designed to be efficient and accessible when finetuning the
model, providing complete code and guidance, making it the best code base upon
which to develop the work. Once the Mercury X1 dataset was imported correctly, it
was a matter of finding the most fitting hyperparameters to correctly finetune the VLA
model. The training has been performed using a single GPU card with 96 gigabytes of
VRAM.
3.3.1 Data filtering and adaptation
After data collection, episodes were postprocessed to align with OpenVLA-OFT’s
input requirements.
This included:
1. Converting each episode to an Hierarchical Data Format version 5 (HDF5)
format to condense all relevant data into a single file
2. Resizing all images to 256×256 pixels, the input resolution required by the
model
3. Packaging the dataset into TensorFlow-compatible files using the OpenVLA-
OFT preprocessing pipeline
This preprocessing ensured that the dataset could be efficiently consumed during the
finetuning phase.
3.3.2 Finetuning Procedure
The finetuning of the OpenVLA-OFT model was conducted using a targeted set of
hyperparameters optimized for training on the Mercury X1 dataset. These parameters
were selected through iterative experimentation, balancing GPU memory constraints,
training stability, and empirical performance, with a particular focus on enabling
generalization in dual-arm manipulation tasks. The training was configured with a
batch size of 4, using image inputs from two synchronized cameras (third-view and
top-down), resized to 256×256 pixels. The learning rate was set to 2 · 10−4, with no
warm-up steps and a decay schedule that reduced the learning rate by a factor of 10
after 75,000 steps. The training was run for a total of 150,005 steps, and a checkpoint
was saved every 20,000 steps to allow evaluation at different training stages. The model
architecture utilized FiLM to condition visual features with language input, improving
the grounding of instructions. Although the OpenVLA-OFT architecture supports
proprioceptive input, it was disabled in this experiment to reduce modality complexity.
Image augmentation was applied to improve visual generalization. The finetuning
also leveraged Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with a rank of 32 and dropout rate of
0.0, allowing efficient adaptation of the large pretrained model without full weight
updates. The LoRA weights were merged during training to ensure compatibility
during inference.
35

The LoRA weights were merged during training to ensure compatibility during inference. 35

pages.text:
The finetuning process was conducted using a single-GPU setup with 96 GB of
VRAM. This hardware configuration was selected in accordance with the memory
and computational requirements outlined in the OpenVLA-OFT repository, which
highlight the substantial demands imposed by its multi-modal transformer architecture.
Using the collected dataset and a modified training configuration, the peak VRAM
usage during finetuning reached approximately 62 GB. This is consistent with the
expected resource footprint for large-scale VLA models trained on rich episodic data.
Following training, the deployment of the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT model was
executed on a consumer-grade GPU with 24 GB of VRAM. During inference, the
model maintained real-time responsiveness while occupying approximately 16 GB
of VRAM, demonstrating the feasibility of running the system in practical settings
without compromising performance. This setup enabled real-world testing on the
Mercury X1 robot and integration within the broader agentic framework.
36
pages.normalizedText: The finetuning process was conducted using a single-GPU setup with 96 GB of VRAM. This hardware configuration was selected in accordance with the memory and computational requirements outlined in the OpenVLA-OFT repository, which highlight the substantial demands imposed by its multi-modal transformer architecture. Using the collected dataset and a modified training configuration, the peak VRAM usage during finetuning reached approximately 62 GB. This is consistent with the expected resource footprint for large-scale VLA models trained on rich episodic data. Following training, the deployment of the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT model was executed on a consumer-grade GPU with 24 GB of VRAM. During inference, the model maintained real-time responsiveness while occupying approximately 16 GB of VRAM, demonstrating the feasibility of running the system in practical settings without compromising performance. This setup enabled real-world testing on the Mercury X1 robot and integration within the broader agentic framework. 36

pages.text:
3.4 Agentic AI Framework implementation
The Agentic AI structure presented in this project is built using the AutoGen framework,
a system designed to manage tool-augmented LLM agents using structured, prompt-
based coordination. At the center of this architecture is a main agent built on LLaMA
3.2 7B [57], which serves as the core reasoning unit and has been chosen considering
its efficiency and performances. This agent does not operate in isolation, but instead
selectively activates specialized tools based on the user’s input. One of the central
ideas in this implementation is to treat the VLA as a callable function. It is invoked by
the main agent only when required, specifically, when the user requests a task that
involves perception of the environment or interaction with it. This makes the system
modular and scalable while maintaining a clear separation between general dialogue
and task execution. When integrated into the Mercury X1 robot platform, this setup
allows the agent to act not only as a conversational assistant but also as a controller
capable of interacting with the real world. This hybrid system, combining language,
vision, and action, is what we refer to as AgenticVLA (see Figure 13), a VLA model
extended with agentic coordination logic and equipped with supporting reasoning and
awareness capabilities.
Figure 13: AgenticVLA complete framework
AutoGen handles input parsing and tool selection by evaluating intent. To do this
effectively, each tool is defined by a specific tool description, which helps the agent
determine which tool to activate in response to a user instruction. The following
sections describe the tools and logic involved.
3.4.1 Conversational Agent
The conversational agent is used whenever the system detects no action-oriented
intent in the input. This means that the user is likely asking a question, seeking

clarification, or issuing a command that does not involve any interaction with the
robot’s environment. The tool uses LLaMA 3.2 7B as its backbone to interpret and
respond to these types of inputs. It acts as the default fallback for general interaction,
allowing the agent to operate like any LLM-based assistant. The Dialogue Tool ensures
that the agent remains useful and responsive, even when the request is not actionable.
System prompt
You are a general-purpose assistant. Your role is to respond to the user’s input
in a manner that is clear, accurate, and informative. Follow these guidelines:
1. Clarity and Relevance:
• Ensure all responses are easy to understand and directly address the
user’s query or instruction.
• Avoid unnecessary elaboration or speculation.
2. Action Boundaries:
• Do not infer or initiate any physical action unless the instruction
contains a clear and explicit intent to interact with the environment.
• If the request is ambiguous or lacks sufficient context, respond with a
clarification request rather than assuming intent.
3. Neutrality and Safety:
• Provide information in a neutral, helpful tone.
• Avoid making assumptions that could lead to incorrect or unsafe
outcomes.
Your objective is to assist effectively while maintaining appropriate boundaries
between language processing and real-world execution.
3.4.2 Act Agent
This agent is responsible for handling all instructions that imply interaction with the
physical environment. When the system detects intent through verbs such as “pick up,”
“move,” or “put,” this agent is triggered. Rather than directly executing a command, the
Act agent first processes the input through two sub-modules: the Reasoning Tool and
the Self-Awareness Tool. These modules allow the system to validate the instruction,
clarify user intent, and ensure the feasibility of the requested action. After this layered
processing, the refined instruction is sent to a server where the OpenVLA-OFT model
is deployed. This model predicts the action sequence that the robot will perform.
39

pages.text:
System prompt
You are a Task Execution Controller. Your role is to interpret user commands
related to physical actions and prepare them for safe and reliable execution by the
robot. Follow these principles:
1. Command Interpretation:
• Parse user instructions related to physical interaction with the environ-
ment.
• Identify intended actions, relevant objects, and spatial targets.
2. Intent and Feasibility Verification:
• Before triggering any robot action, use integrated reasoning and
perception tools to verify:
– The clarity of the user’s intent,
– The presence and state of required objects in the environment,
and
– The feasibility and safety of the action.
3. Controlled Execution:
• Only authorize actions that are fully validated by reasoning and per-
ception components.
• If validation fails or the instruction is ambiguous, defer execution and
return a clarification request to the user.
Your objective is to ensure that all physical commands are executed safely,
accurately, and only when the conditions for success are fully satisfied.
Reasoning Tool
The Reasoning Tool addresses a key limitation of current VLAs: their restricted
ability to generalize from natural language. Since many VLAs must be trained on
highly structured commands, they can fail when given ambiguous, compound, or
loosely phrased instructions. This tool operates as an interpreter that rewrites or
restructures user input into forms that the VLA has been trained to understand. It also
decomposes high-level commands into smaller, step-by-step tasks that the model can
handle individually. This is especially useful in scenarios involving multiple objects,
complex sequences, or uncommon phrasing.
40

pages.text:
LLaMA 3.2 7B is used here to perform the reasoning and rephrasing step, ensuring
alignment between human language and the robot’s capabilities.
System prompt
You are a reasoning layer. Your role is to translate user instructions into precise,
structured tasks that a robot can understand and execute reliably. Follow these
guidelines:
1. Decompose complex commands into smaller, sequential steps that reflect
the capabilities of the robot.
2. Each final task must be formatted as:
put the [color] [object] on the [color] plate
where [color] and [object] are specific descriptors present in the scene.
3. Verify availability:
• If any referenced object or plate color is not confirmed to be present in
the scene, do not guess.
• Instead, query the perception agent or another available agent to
confirm whether the object exists in the environment before proceeding
with the action plan.
4. Maintain clarity and precision. Avoid ambiguous references or assumptions
about the environment.
Your output should only contain valid robot-actionable instructions that strictly
follow the above format and logic.
Self-awareness Tool
The Self-Awareness Tool helps the system understand its environment and evaluate
whether a requested action is feasible. This component uses visual input from a
third-person camera to generate a description of the current scene and monitor the
execution of tasks over time.
Using Gemma 3 12B from Google DeepMind [58] as the visual backbone, the tool
performs two essential functions. First, it identifies the objects currently visible to
the robot, providing the agent with a list of available items in the workspace. Second,
it compares frames across time to detect potential failures during execution. For
instance, if the robot was holding a cup in one frame but has dropped it in the next, the
tool can detect the failure and notify the system.
41

pages.text:
This layer adds robustness to the VLA pipeline, enabling the robot to avoid unsafe or
impossible actions and to react to failures when they occur.
System prompt
You are a Visual Scene Monitor responsible for analyzing third-person camera
input to assess the state and progression of robotic tasks. Your duties include:
1. Scene Interpretation:
• Continuously observe the workspace using third-person visual input.
• Identify and list all visible objects, including their type, color, and
spatial location relative to the workspace.
2. Task Outcome Verification:
• Analyze a sequence of frames over time to determine whether the robot
has successfully completed its assigned task.
• Use positional and state changes of objects to verify correctness.
3. Anomaly Detection and Reporting:
• Detect and report any inconsistencies, such as missing objects, incor-
rect placements, or unanticipated object movements.
• Flag any errors, such as dropped items, collisions, or task deviations.
• Identify unexpected changes in object state (e.g., orientation, damage)
or environment layout.
Your output must be structured, objective, and based solely on observable visual
evidence. Do not make assumptions beyond what is visible in the input frames.
3.5 Framework summary
Figure 13 shows how a user instruction is handled by the Agentic AI system. The LLM
agent first interprets the input. If the request includes an actionable verb (e.g., "pick
up"), the Act agent is triggered. The Reasoning Tool reformulates the instruction into
an executable form. Then, the Self-Awareness Tool verifies that the action is feasible
by analyzing the scene captured from a third-view camera. Finally, the instruction is
sent to a backend model (OpenVLA-OFT), which predicts the precise set of actions
for the robot to execute.
42

pages.text:
4 Experiments and Results
In this section, the experiments conducted are introduced and discussed. Each
experiment is structured as an A vs B vs C evaluation (see Figure 14), comparing
three models:
• OpenVLA: Serving as the initial experiment to develop a functioning VLA for
the Mercury X1 robot, OpenVLA was trained from scratch by combining the
OpenX-Embodiment dataset with the custom Mercury X1 dataset. This training
began from the Prismatic 7B VLM [59], finetuning its vision encoder, projector,
and final LLM layer. The approach aimed to preserve the generalist knowledge
from existing datasets while adapting the model to the specific characteristics of
the new robotic setup.
• Finetuned OpenVLA-OFT: Obtained by finetuning the OpenVLA model on
the Mercury X1 dataset using LoRA, this version is more specialized for the
specific robot and tasks.
• AgenticVLA: Building upon the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT, AgenticVLA inte-
grates the model into the Agentic AI framework introduced earlier. It benefits
from enhanced intelligence through tools like the Reasoning Tool and the
Self-Awareness Tool.
The objective of these experiments is to evaluate the performance of the VLA model
in robotic control tasks, particularly assessing its capabilities when applied to setups
or robots it has not previously encountered. This evaluation focuses on understanding
the model’s sensitivity to input variations and its robustness across different task
conditions, using a single robotic platform. Additionally, the experiments aim to
compare the baseline OpenVLA model against implementations based on the Agentic
AI framework to investigate potential differences in performance across general
embodied AI tasks.
Figure 14: Summary of the models used for the experiments.
By conducting these comparisons, the study seeks to provide a comprehensive, unbiased
assessment of how architectural variations influence task success and adaptability in
real-world robotic applications.
43

pages.text:
4.1 Evaluation metrics
To evaluate the experiments, both quantitative and qualitative metrics were defined.
Since the VLA predicts a positional vector (7 dimensions for each arm, plus gripper), it
is possible to compare these outputs to ground truth values collected during benchmark
creation. In other words, three test datasets were recorded to gather trajectories that
can be compared with those predicted by the embodied system. This means that
the same language instruction was given to the real system operating in the physical
environment, and the resulting trajectories were collected and stored to evaluate model
performance. The comparison is performed by plotting, over time, the coordinates for
each arm and the gripper values, as well as analyzing the Success Rate (SR) and the
average Mean Squared Error (MSE). The SR is defined as the ratio between the number
of successfully completed tasks, where the robot was able to complete the intended
action, and the total number of tasks performed. The average MSE, instead, measured
in millimeters, is calculated by averaging the MSE across the relevant dimensions (x,
y, z, grasp) for each arm. Formally, given the predicted trajectory yˆ = [𝑦ˆ1, 𝑦ˆ2, . . . , 𝑦ˆ𝑇 ]
and the ground truth trajectory y = [𝑦1, 𝑦2, . . . , 𝑦𝑇 ], where each 𝑦𝑡 ∈ R𝑑 represents
the vector of relevant positions and gripper values at time 𝑡, the MSE is defined as:
MSE = 1
𝑇 × 𝑑
𝑇	∑︁
𝑡=1
𝑑	∑︁
𝑖=1
(𝑦𝑡,𝑖 − 𝑦ˆ𝑡,𝑖)2
where 𝑇 is the total number of time steps, and 𝑑 is the dimensionality of the output
vector (e.g., 4 for each arm’s position and gripper). This single value represents the
overall deviation of each episode’s trajectory from the ground truth. This evaluation
approach aligns with the experimental procedure adopted by other research institutes,
such as the creators of the OpenVLA model [3].
4.2 Experiment benchmarks
Each benchmark was designed to evaluate a specific aspect of how VLA models
perform in embodied robotic tasks. The first benchmark examines the influence of
different natural language instructions on task execution, assessing whether variations in
phrasing affect the robot’s behavior. The second benchmark continues this investigation
under more constrained prompting conditions, probing the limitations of instruction-
based control. The third benchmark focuses on the system’s ability to interpret its
surroundings and respond appropriately to changes in the scene, testing perception-
grounding capabilities. The first and third benchmarks were each carried out over
100 episodes, while the second included 90 episodes. Performance was evaluated
using Success Rate (SR) and average Mean Squared Error (MSE). All scenes were
constructed with distinct object instances, only one per category, to minimize ambiguity
in task execution.
44

pages.text:
4.2.1 Semantic relationship understanding
The first benchmark introduced was designed to evaluate whether VLAs are capable
of learning correlations between similar objects and performing multiple elemen-
tary movements to complete a task of higher complexity. In this experiment, the
instruction given to each model is: "put the fruits on the plate". The scene includes
two fruits that can be picked up, a banana and grapes, both of which are familiar to
the VLA, as they are part of the training set (see Figure 15). This scenario requires
the model to predict a more complex trajectory: picking up the first object from the
table and placing it on the plate, then sequentially repeating the same steps with
the second object. Tests were conducted using both arms of the robot, although
the same arm was used consistently for each task. This task can be considered a
long-horizon robotic manipulation challenge, compared to the simpler examples used
during model finetuning, and represents a significant difficulty for autonomous systems.
As shown in Table 7, this experiment highlights an important limitation of the
OpenVLA model. Both the original OpenVLA and the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT
demonstrate that these models struggle to understand task sequentiality and object
relationships. In fact, when instructed to "pick up the fruits," OpenVLA-OFT moved
toward an object it was familiar with, in this case either the banana or the grapes,
and successfully placed it on the plate. However, once the first object was placed, the
task was considered complete, and the second fruit was never interacted with. The
AgenticVLA, on the other hand, follows the same principle but with an additional layer
of understanding. It splits the instruction "pick up the fruits" into "pick up the banana"
and "pick up the grapes," making it easier for the finetuned model to understand what
it needs to do. For this reason, after the first object was placed on the plate, a new
instruction was fed to the system so that the complete task could be executed.
Figure 15: Image recording of AgenticVLA model succesfully performing the task
"pick up the fruits"
The success rate of AgenticVLA was 44%, while the other models were not able to
accomplish this level of complexity. The failures observed with AgenticVLA were
mainly due to issues such as dropping the object, failing to pick it up correctly, or an
45

Both the original OpenVLA and the finetuned OpenVLA-OFT demonstrate that these models struggle to understand task sequentiality and object relationships. In fact, when instructed to "pick up the fruits," OpenVLA-OFT moved toward an object it was familiar with, in this case either the banana or the grapes, and successfully placed it on the plate. However, once the first object was placed, the task was considered complete, and the second fruit was never interacted with. The AgenticVLA, on the other hand, follows the same principle but with an additional layer of understanding. It splits the instruction "pick up the fruits" into "pick up the banana" and "pick up the grapes," making it easier for the finetuned model to understand what it needs to do. For this reason, after the first object was placed on the plate, a new instruction was fed to the system so that the complete task could be executed. To better understand the behavior of each model, the trajectory of a representative

the one used in this work.
While these results may appear trivial or even expected, they underscore a critical
and often overlooked limitation: despite being trained on a large and diverse set
of robot demonstrations, models like OpenVLA are not readily transferable to new
robotic embodiments or control contexts. Given its training on semantically rich
language-action pairs, OpenVLA is expected to infer spatial and functional relation-
ships between objects in the scene. However, this is not reflected in its behavior
when applied to a previously unseen dual-arm setup. This finding emphasizes that
such models, despite their scale and pretraining, cannot yet be considered general-
purpose controllers without targeted adaptation or finetuning. Rather than treating
this outcome as a failure of the model, it highlights the necessity of developing
strategies, such as the AgenticVLA framework, to bridge the gap between pretrained
vision-language models and the operational demands of embodied, real, world robotics.

4.2.2 Prompt complexity analysis
This benchmark examines how VLAs are influenced by the complexity of the prompt.
To explore this, the experiment was designed to analyze performance as the input
instruction increases in complexity, by varying the syntax that express intent and
adding redundant information. Three different text instructions were chosen:
• Easy: "put banana on plate". This instruction is exactly as the one used to
finetune the model and it has been chosen to work as the baseline for this
experiment.
• Medium: "move banana from table to plate". In this case, few tweaks has been
added, changing the main intent verb and specifying where the object must be
picked up from.
• Hard: "if possible, please pick up the banana from surface and place it on the
plate" This instruction introduces different level of complexity. Firstly, the if
cause and secondly it changes all the intent verbs again.
The goal of this experiment is to compare the two models when given different prompts,
highlighting the sensitivity VLAs have to input instructions and demonstrating how
powerful and flexible the AgenticVLA is when equipped with functions like the
Reasoning Tool in these scenarios.
The results of the second experiment aligned with expectations, showing that increasing
the complexity of the text instruction does not help the VLA better understand the
goal. As shown in Table 8 and Figure 15, in the simple case where both the finetuned
OpenVLA-OFT and AgenticVLA received the same prompt, "put banana on plate,"
