4.2.3 Self-awareness capabilities
The third and final benchmark examines how VLAs behave when asked to interact with
objects that are not present in the scene. This scenario differs from the previous two and
aims to demonstrate how the VLA model lacks understanding of the scene, highlighting
the need for an additional layer of comprehension, such as the Self-awareness tool. In
this experiment, the instruction given to the system is "put banana on plate," but for
the first three seconds, the banana is not visible from the robot’s point of view (see
Figure 22). After three seconds, the banana is introduced into the scene. The goal is
to observe how the VLA model reacts and whether it continues trying to pick up an
object that is not yet present.
This benchmark highlights how VLAs struggle to understand what is happening in
the scene. The trajectory plot provides valuable insights into the model’s behavior.
As shown in Figure 23, it is clear that as soon as the task begins, the model tends to
predict a trajectory that moves the arm toward the table surface, regardless of whether
the object is present or not, causing the robot to remain stuck in that position until the
banana is placed in the scene. Once the banana appears, the VLA is able to localize
the object and often successfully completes the task, achieving a 43% SR. However,
in other cases, when the robot rushes to pick up something prematurely, it becomes
stuck, failing to recognize the banana’s position and thus failing the task. In contrast,
the AgenticVLA performs well thanks to the use of the Self-Awareness tool. This tool
ensures that the task is performed only when the banana is present in the scene; until
then, it keeps the robot in the rest position. This simple yet effective mechanism boosts
the model’s performance to an 85% SR, similar to the results seen in the previous
benchmark.
Figure 22: Image recording of AgenticVLA model succesfully performing the task
"put banana on plate" with the banana being introduced in the scene after 3 seconds.

4.3 Discussion
This section evaluates the reliability and validity of the experimental findings and
reflects on the practical and theoretical implications of the work. While the results
demonstrate clear advantages for the AgenticVLA system, it is important to consider
both strengths and limitations with a balanced and transparent perspective.
4.3.1 Reliability and Internal Validity
The experimental pipeline was designed with a strong emphasis on consistency and
methodological rigor. A custom-built teleoperation system based on the Meta Quest
3 enabled immersive, high-frequency, and repeatable data acquisition. The use of a
standardized RLDS data format ensured compatibility with modern VLA training
pipelines and supported a structured and efficient data flow. The data collection
process was carefully controlled. Task instructions were predefined, sensor inputs
were synchronized across multiple viewpoints, and a uniform sampling frequency
was applied to all episodes. These design choices contributed to a high degree of
internal reliability, ensuring that data was consistent across trials and reproducible
under similar conditions. Regarding internal validity, the task design focused on a
well-defined pick and place scenario with clearly observable success criteria and
structured instructions. This approach made it possible to evaluate model behavior in a
controlled setting. While the environment was intentionally limited to maintain clarity
and reduce variability, the experiments still offer valuable insights into how both the
VLA and AgenticVLA systems process multi-modal inputs and generate meaningful
actions.
4.3.2 External Validity and Generalizability
From the perspective of external validity, the study establishes a solid foundation but
also highlights areas for future extension. The use of the Mercury X1 humanoid robot
in a laboratory environment allowed for close control over variables such as lighting,
object positioning, and sensor configuration. This level of control helped minimize
confusing factors and enabled a focused evaluation of system performance. However,
the controlled nature of the environment also introduces natural limitations. The results
presented may not directly generalize to different robot embodiments, unseen physical
layouts, or more complex task domains without further adaptation or retraining.
In particular, changes in camera angles, object properties, or ambient conditions
could affect the system’s perception and decision-making processes. Despite these
constraints, the AgenticVLA system demonstrated promising robustness in handling
variations in language phrasing and task formulation. This flexibility suggests that the
framework is well suited for extensions that involve broader instruction sets, real-world
variability, or more dynamic interaction scenarios.
54

4.3.3 Evaluation of OpenVLA Performance
A key component of this thesis was the evaluation of the standalone OpenVLA and
OpenVLA-OFT models. Despite being trained and finetuned on a dedicated dual-
arm dataset, these models did not achieve satisfactory performance in the proposed
experimental setting. Several factors may help explain this outcome:
• Mismatch between architecture and setup: Although OpenVLA-OFT supports
multi-view inputs and dual-arm setups, the specific configuration of the Mercury
X1 robot, combined with two camera perspectives and language-based task
definitions, may have presented an unfamiliar and challenging distribution of
inputs. This complexity could have limited the model’s ability to generalize
effectively.
• Lack of contextual filtering and intent verification: OpenVLA executes
instructions reactively without verifying task feasibility, environmental context,
or possible ambiguities in language. As a result, it lacks a feedback mechanism
to adjust or refine behavior when inputs are unclear or unexpected.
• Fixed interpretation of instructions: VLA models like OpenVLA rely heavily
on the structure of the input prompt. Variations in phrasing or minor contextual
differences can lead to inconsistent outputs, especially in manipulation tasks
that require precise spatial reasoning.
Despite these limitations, including OpenVLA in the evaluation was important for
two key reasons:
• It served as a baseline: Comparing the AgenticVLA system against OpenVLA
highlighted the performance improvements enabled by the agentic architecture.
This provided a concrete measure of how additional reasoning and modular task
delegation can improve robustness and success rate.
• It exposed architectural boundaries: The limited performance of OpenVLA
reaffirmed the importance of integrating reactive models within broader control
frameworks. VLA systems alone, while powerful, benefit substantially from
structured decision-making processes to operate reliably in complex or novel
environments.
In summary, the OpenVLA model played a valuable role in this work, not as a definitive
solution, but as a point of comparison that motivated the design and implementation
of more adaptive, agentic control systems.
4.3.4 Interpretation of AgenticVLA Improvements
The performance of the AgenticVLA system showed clear improvements in task
completion and instruction interpretation. These results are encouraging and suggest
that combining VLA models with a structured agentic framework adds value to

embodied AI systems. Most of the observed improvements stemmed not from changes
in the low-level control capabilities of the VLA model, but rather from the ability of the
agent framework to filter input, decompose tasks, and verify conditions before action
execution. In other words, the model did not become better at manipulation itself,
but became more effective at deciding what to do and when to act. This distinction
highlights a central contribution of this thesis. The agentic framework acts as an
intelligent wrapper around existing models, allowing for more robust and context-
aware behavior without requiring major changes to the underlying policy model. This
approach is particularly relevant in light of recent trends in robotics and AI, where
modularity, composability, and interpretability are becoming key design principles.
By demonstrating that it is possible to combine VLAs and agentic components in a
cohesive and working prototype, this thesis shows that these research directions are
not only compatible but mutually reinforcing. The presented system offers a concrete
example of how general-purpose AI models can be deployed more effectively when
integrated into structured control pipelines.

pages.text:
5 Conclusions
5.1 Summary of Findings
This thesis investigated whether integrating Agentic AI frameworks with VLA mod-
els could enhance autonomy, adaptability, and context-awareness in embodied AI
systems. The study also explored the potential of distributed, modular agents for
robotic applications, focusing on both single-agent and multi-agent architectures.
The results demonstrated that a purely reactive model like OpenVLA, even when
fine-tuned on a custom dual-arm dataset, struggles with generalization and task
execution in unfamiliar setups. In contrast, the AgenticVLA framework, where the
VLA is deployed as a tool within a reasoning agent, was able to increase task suc-
cess rates, especially in cases involving ambiguous or structurally complex instructions.
However, it is important to note that the performance improvements observed were
primarily due to the agent’s control over when and how the VLA model was activated,
rather than improvements in the manipulation policy itself. This highlights a key
finding: the agentic layer primarily enhances the system’s robustness and input
handling, not low-level motor competence.
5.2 Addressing the Research Questions
The findings allow us to provide the following responses to the research questions:
• RQ1: Can an Agentic AI framework be combined with a Visual-Language-
Action model towards achieving AGI?
While this work does not claim to advance toward AGI in any general sense,
the AgenticVLA system shows that embedding a VLA model within an agen-
tic control framework increases system flexibility, contextual awareness, and
decision-making autonomy. This confirms that such integration is both feasible
and beneficial, especially in cases where VLAs on their own are insufficiently
robust. However, this approach still requires further development in memory,
long-horizon planning, and self-adaptation to be considered a substantial step
toward general intelligence.
• RQ2: Can a network of AI agents improve the perception and decision-
making capabilities of autonomous robots in a collaborative task setting?
If so, how?
This thesis implemented a modular agentic system composed of multiple
coordinated agents, each responsible for a distinct function such as reasoning,
tool selection, and task execution. While this does not constitute a fully distributed
multi-agent system across devices or embodiments, the architecture already
demonstrates the value of internal agent specialization and coordination. The use
of AutoGen enabled structured communication between agents and facilitated
clear separation of roles. The results suggest that structuring control through
modular, interacting agents can improve both perception and decision-making.
57
pages.normalizedText: 5 Conclusions 5.1 Summary of Findings This thesis investigated whether integrating Agentic AI frameworks with VLA models could enhance autonomy, adaptability, and context-awareness in embodied AI systems. The study also explored the potential of distributed, modular agents for robotic applications, focusing on both single-agent and multi-agent architectures. The results demonstrated that a purely reactive model like OpenVLA, even when fine-tuned on a custom dual-arm dataset, struggles with generalization and task execution in unfamiliar setups. In contrast, the AgenticVLA framework, where the VLA is deployed as a tool within a reasoning agent, was able to increase task success rates, especially in cases involving ambiguous or structurally complex instructions. However, it is important to note that the performance improvements observed were primarily due to the agent’s control over when and how the VLA model was activated, rather than improvements in the manipulation policy itself. This highlights a key finding: the agentic layer primarily enhances the system’s robustness and input handling, not low-level motor competence. 5.2 Addressing the Research Questions The findings allow us to provide the following responses to the research questions: • RQ1: Can an Agentic AI framework be combined with a Visual-LanguageAction model towards achieving AGI? While this work does not claim to advance toward AGI in any general sense, the AgenticVLA system shows that embedding a VLA model within an agentic control framework increases system flexibility, contextual awareness, and decision-making autonomy. This confirms that such integration is both feasible and beneficial, especially in cases where VLAs on their own are insufficiently robust. However, this approach still requires further development in memory, long-horizon planning, and self-adaptation to be considered a substantial step toward general intelligence. • RQ2: Can a network of AI agents improve the perception and decisionmaking capabilities of autonomous robots in a collaborative task setting? If so, how? This thesis implemented a modular agentic system composed of multiple coordinated agents, each responsible for a distinct function such as reasoning, tool selection, and task execution. While this does not constitute a fully distributed multi-agent system across devices or embodiments, the architecture already demonstrates the value of internal agent specialization and coordination. The use of AutoGen enabled structured communication between agents and facilitated clear separation of roles. The results suggest that structuring control through modular, interacting agents can improve both perception and decision-making. 57

pages.text:
Future work should extend this architecture toward more decentralized settings,
investigating inter-agent communication across physical systems, collaborative
task arbitration, and dynamic resource allocation in networked environments.
5.3 Modularity and the Role of Specialized Agents
One of the main contributions of this work is the demonstration of a modular control
pipeline where individual capabilities, such as perception, reasoning, and language
understanding, are handled by distinct components. By deploying the VLA model as
a callable tool, rather than embedding it in a monolithic policy, the system allows for
flexible task decomposition and controlled resource use. This modularity supports the
idea of using multiple smaller VLAs, each specialized for a certain type of perception
or embodiment, within a networked system of intelligent agents. Such systems
could dynamically allocate resources based on task demands, robot embodiment, or
environmental context. This architecture aligns with recent developments in distributed
AI and offers a promising direction for scalable and adaptive robotics.
5.4 Open Challenges and Future Research Directions
The work presented in this thesis raises several important questions for future research:
• How can multi-agent systems coordinate specialized VLAs for collabora-
tive execution? What mechanisms are required to ensure coherence, prevent
redundancy, and resolve conflicts among distributed modules?
• To what extent can agentic frameworks generalize to complex, real-world
environments? This includes handling dynamic or noisy sensory input, open-
ended task definitions, and unstructured language.
• What is the minimal structure required to maintain safe and effective
agent behavior in modular systems? As systems become more distributed and
compositional, ensuring consistency and interpretability becomes increasingly
important.
• How can agentic architectures incorporate long-term memory, state persis-
tence, or experience replay? Current implementations operate in an essentially
stateless manner, limiting performance in multi-step or multi-session scenarios.
5.5 Final Remarks
This thesis has shown that Agentic AI frameworks can partially compensate for the
limitations of reactive VLA models, particularly by improving instruction interpretation
and decision control. The proposed AgenticVLA architecture represents a step toward
more modular, context-aware robotic intelligence. While the system remains limited in
scope and robustness, it provides a functional demonstration of how agents, tools, and
reasoning layers can be integrated into a unified embodied AI pipeline. The findings
